body:
### Describe the bug

I assume the idea of `list[cython.int]` is that the result of indexing it is a C int. 

### Code to reproduce the behaviour:

```cython
import cython

def f(l: list[cython.int]):
    l0 = l[0]
    print(l0*2)
```

This generates the C code:

```c
__pyx_t_1 = __Pyx_GetItemInt_List(__pyx_v_l, 0, long, 1, __Pyx_PyLong_From_long, 0, 1, 1, __Pyx_ReferenceSharing_FunctionArgument); if (unlikely(!__pyx_t_1)) __PYX_ERR(0, 4, __pyx_L1_error)
  __Pyx_GOTREF(__pyx_t_1);
  __pyx_t_2 = __Pyx_PyLong_As_int(__pyx_t_1); if (unlikely((__pyx_t_2 == (int)-1) && PyErr_Occurred())) __PYX_ERR(0, 4, __pyx_L1_error)
  __Pyx_DECREF(__pyx_t_1); __pyx_t_1 = 0;
  __pyx_t_1 = __Pyx_PyLong_From_int(__pyx_t_2); if (unlikely(!__pyx_t_1)) __PYX_ERR(0, 4, __pyx_L1_error)
  __Pyx_GOTREF(__pyx_t_1);

  if (__Pyx_PyInt_FromNumber(&__pyx_t_1, NULL, 0) < (0)) __PYX_ERR(0, 4, __pyx_L1_error)
  __pyx_v_l0 = ((PyObject*)__pyx_t_1);
  __pyx_t_1 = 0;
```

* `l0` appears to be inferred as a python int (probably... it might just be a PyObject).
* The list is index to produce a Python object.
* The Python object is converted to a C int.
* The C int is converted back to to a Python int.
* For some reason `__Pyx_PyInt_FromNumber` is called to do a bit more work on the Python int.

### Expected behaviour

Something efficient, either:
* `l0` inferred as `cython.int`, one conversion
* `l0` inferred as a Python object, double conversion via C int optimized out.

In this case the first version is better, but in other cases the second version might be better.

### OS

Linux

### Python version

3.13

### Cython version

Current master

### Additional context

I'm not completely convinced that `list[cython.int]` is a sensible thing to support because I think it may mislead users about what they're storing. But that's probably separate

comment_data.body:
It's marked as unsafe because you are multiplying by 2.

We might be setting an expression node type where we only want to infer it. The conversion should then be done by the assignment.
Yes true - if I remove the *2 then it looks a lot better.

From my intuition, this code might be problematic:
https://github.com/cython/cython/blob/48a3fdabfaec30682ae02b1afb385a54b88a4852/Cython/Compiler/ExprNodes.py#L4517-L4521
Note that this isn't in `infer_type` (where inferring the item type would be perfectly fine) but in `analyse_as_pyobject`. Coercion is something that should come from outside, not from inside as it happens here. The type that we read from the container is really `py_object_type`, not necessarily the type that we are coercing to here. Whether the coerced type is used or at all helpful is not this node's decision.
Yes, removing that code solves this problem but lets the tests fail. I'll look into it.
@matusvalo

I think the code section above needs to be removed and then it's mostly the tests that are wrong here. They mostly test the type inference but are not written to allow it. We should
- move them out of the variable annotations test modules and into a new `type_inference_generics.py` test module (or more than one, if necessary)
- change the tests' usages of `typeof(…expression…)` into an assignment to a new local (type-inferred) variable and a `typeof()` of that variable to make actual use of type inference for the tests evaluation

The problem is that something like `typeof(c[3])` takes the type of the container indexing expression, which is always a Python type because it's a Python container. Only the usage (such as an assignment) can then make use of an inferred type, but the inferred type is not the real type of the value. It's just what triggers the coercion to the right type.

(We should wait for https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7728 to be merged first.)

> I think the code section above needs to be removed

When I checked the generated code, basically it is identical to version with plain `l: list`. Is then any benefit at all? I am not telling that there is not an issue but my point is that just removing will end up with the code having no optimizations. Or I am understanding it wrong? Based on sentence:

> Only the usage (such as an assignment) can then make use of an inferred type

I understand thaht that we need to introduce new points of inference/coercion into the nodes "consuming" the index node...

> (We should wait for https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7728 to be merged first.)

And following too: #7719

Hmm. Some thoughts from my side.

Our type inference is only applied at the assignment level, i.e. to variables and not to partial expressions. Therefore, if we want to use an inferred type inside of an expression, we need to make it the type of the partial expression that creates it.

If users choose a C type as generic item type of a container, it's their deliberate choice and Cython should obey. Thus, we can try to avoid useless back and forth conversions as in the case above, but in the end, it was the users choice to type the result of iteration and indexing for the container, so that's what comes out of it.

I was always reluctant to eliminate seemingly redundant C-Python-C coercions because they also discard any value range checks when the two C (numeric) types on both ends have different sizes. During the coercion back from Python to C, the conversion function would fail if the value is out of bounds. In the original example above, the double conversion is not entirely redundant because values that are out of bounds for a C `int` are detected and rejected by the `__Pyx_PyLong_As_int()` call. That safety goes away if we do a direct C-to-C "conversion" via a cast (or even simple assignment) instead. Also, some coercions are special, e.g. `CoerceIntToBytesNode`, and thus cannot be replaced by a C-to-C cast or conversion.

I also think that we may not always distinguish between a user enforced coercion and one that we injected internally. There are a few cases, for example, where analysing a `TypecastNode` directly returns a coercion node, without remembering the explicit cast. That makes it somewhat difficult to determine if a C-Py-C coercion that we find is accidental or if the user explicitly said so, e.g. via some construct like `cdef int x = <object> c_int_value` (there are probably reasons why this wouldn't be completely insane, including range checking).

We could introduce our own C value range checks for C-Py-C conversion cases, though. Like adding a special `CoerceCIntegerNode` that takes a C integer value and a C integer target type as argument and that generates code to raise a Python exception if the value is outside of the target type's range. That might even increase the safety of current code, although at the cost of additional runtime checks (and exceptions potentially popping up in unexpected places, etc.).

No real conclusion from my side yet. What are your thoughts on this?

> If users choose a C type as generic item type of a container, it's their deliberate choice and Cython should obey. Thus, we can try to avoid useless back and forth conversions as in the case above, but in the end, it was the users choice to type the result of iteration and indexing for the container, so that's what comes out of it.

I think I believe this argument. I suspect there will be some people who just do `from cython import int` with the intention of making all `int` annotations C ints. But again, that's their choice.

There might be an argument for marking user-requested coercions as special. I'm not sure I'd have immediately identified this one as user-requested though.

I don't really think it's worth adding range checks in generally (i.e. the `CoerceCIntegerNode`) - it seems like they'll either pop up in unexpected places and surprise people or they'll fail to pop up and surprise people.

> This bug **does not** happen when directive `cython: infer_types = True` is set.

That's because the test case specifically creates a situation (unsafe integer `*2`) that distinguishes our default (safe) type inference from full (`infer_types=True`) type inference. I'm sure that other cases can be constructed where even full inference ends up with inefficient code, maybe when using the indexing result inside of certain expressions.

It's also not just type inference. When users assign the indexing result to an object typed variable, or pass it into a function that expected an object argument, we'd end up with the same back and forth conversion as it stands. The issue is really that we have a Python value in our hands but mark it as whatever C type the user told us, and then work from that without looking back. `CoerceToPyTypeNode` does not look through `CoerceFromPyTypeNode`, that's why this happens.

Note that there is already specific code to undo back and forth conversion that appears during the builtins optimisation phase. Looking over it, it might just be by accident that it doesn't strike here. And it should normally end up dropping integer range checks as well, so there's precedent.
https://github.com/cython/cython/blob/02dc3d415aaf44dc740fb7ddea476c69c01bf438/Cython/Compiler/Optimize.py#L2382-L2386
EDIT: I just noticed that it's simply working the wrong way round, it checks for the C-Py-C case, not the Py-C-Py case that we have here.

Proposal: We could let `CoerceToPyTypeNode` and `CoerceFromPyTypeNode` know of each other and generally undo back and forth coercions, in valid cases, when their respective `.coerce_to()` method is called.

What do you think?

> What do you think?

I think it makes sense in general to eliminate doubled coercions. I already have simple & stupid PoC for that [1] but not finished because tests are failing with this PoC.

In any case I am still puzzled what is the conclusion for several comments:
1. should be used `coercion()` used in `analyse_as_pyobject()`? I think your argument makes sense that coercion should be used in "consuming nodes" - ref. https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/7729#issuecomment-4583205293
2. Related to point 1. Should be given snippet removed and coercion removed for direct expression use - ref. https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/7729#issuecomment-4583410709
3. What is an agreement on @da-woods comment - ref. *Additional Context* in the description of this PR.

For me, I see the biggest improvement in Cython of the support of the subscripted types is for pyobjects (str, extension classes etc.) where we can infer type and optimize out access to given pyobject. For C types the benefit is diluted by the need of converting from pyobject and also that, as @da-woods pointed out, `list[cython.int]` is not list of C integers as the type hints.

That said I prefer to:
1. Fix and allow first class support in cython for pyobjects inside containers
2. Think/discuss about possible C types support

In any case in the meanwhile I will slowly work on eliminating doubled coercion as proposed by @scoder .

[1] https://github.com/matusvalo/cython/tree/refs/heads/skip_py_c_py

My comment on the validity of `list[cython.int]`.

Maybe we shouldn't worry too much on what *is stored*. Because we don't control that, we can't tell from the outside, and it could change. For example I believe PyPy does actually store lists of C ints in some cases. 

So it's more about if it makes sense to infer it from variables that come from the list or for Cython to add the conversation automatically.

I might go and look at how Numpy dtypes are usually used in typing. They're probably the closest things to C integers in Python typing. I don't know that very well right now.

I don't think it should be a warning though - I think it should either be supported or not.

My take on this is that `list[cython.int]` etc. seems a reasonable type annotation with the intention to _use_ the extracted item values as `cython.int`. We should generally allow that.

This ticket suggests an _optimisation_ for the case that the declared (C) type does not fit a specific usage and leads to inefficient code, maybe just in a few of all usages in a function. It's not a strict requirement to resolve this issue in order to support the general feature, but we should avoid the useless inefficiency if we can, in order to relieve our users from having to intervene manually.

As far as I can see, there are two observable behaviours that an optimisation should not break. One is failures to convert, i.e. cases where the original Python value cannot be converted to the declared C type. We should try not to let this case pass silently. This also includes `None` item values, but not only.

The second case is about "type cleaning". A Py-C-Py conversion always gives a plain Python builtin type in the end, regardless of the exact type of the original Python value. It might have been a subtype of a builtin type, or an arbitrary user defined number-like class, it doesn't matter. In the end, once we passed through a C value, we lost that information and continue with a bare builtin type. That's probably also what our type inference expects, and we must definitely not break our own type inference for an optimisation.

Since we're still designing a new type declaration feature, there is a chance we can get away with documenting caveats rather than creating behaviour. But that should be a conscious decision. Making behaviour intuitive is always better than documenting quirks somewhere.

I could imagine generating a simple per-type C utility function for the conversion. It'd probably be sufficient to generate one per C-type, although (Python) user target type declarations could be taken into account mostly for free. If the original Python value has the exact expected builtin type, it would pass it through immediately (preferably even from a wrapper macro), and for everything else, we can afford to do something more costly, like actually converting the value through C, or calling `PyNumber_Int()` etc. A conversion through C would probably be efficient enough in most cases and assure the C value range.

For code that uses `list[cython.int]`, I would expect the Python values in the list to be Python `int`s in >90% of the real-world cases, so the fallback conversion code would be barely used and mostly be there for semantic reasons.

Yes - agree it's not a defect but just somewhat inefficient code.

> For code that uses list[cython.int], I would expect the Python values in the list to be Python ints in >90% of the real-world cases, so the fallback conversion code would be barely used and mostly be there for semantic reasons.

You might _sometimes_ get things like the Numpy fixed-size scalar types. But they'd definitely be much less common.

-----------------------------------

To me, the main advantage of making sure that the two "observable behaviours" always happen is that nobody will be surpised if they get turned on and off as a result of a new optimization.

They type check feels like the more important of the two

> The second case is about "type cleaning". [...]

That seems more like a consequence of the Py-C-Py conversion than something that's obviously wanted. For example, I don't see why it necessarily needs to happen for a list of `numpy.int64` or `ctypes.int` or similar

I can see that someone shouldn't rely on getting a specific PyObject out of a `list[cython.int]` because clearly it *might* go through a C int but the cleaning doesn't feel like we need to guarantee it one way or another. (I'd guess most of the time it's probably cheaper and easier to implement to just do it though)

> the cleaning doesn't feel like we need to guarantee it one way or another

We can't infer Python `int` for the items of `list[cython.int]` if we don't clean the type. Going through a C `int` allows the list items to be arbitrary objects with an `.__index__` method. We'd have to infer `object` in order to represent that correctly.

Again, that's just type inference and nothing is inherently wrong if we infer `object`. It's just a bit annoying and potentially surprising for users as most of them will only ever see the 'obvious' case of Python `int`s in the list.

title: [BUG] `list[cython.int]` generates working but bad code
